findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion for `near_cap_count` to AC1 (e.g. 'near_cap_count equals the number of fixture modules whose LOC falls within 10% of the fixture cap') so the field's computation is covered by the acceptance test.
evidence: Return dict is `{"count", "near_cap_count", "over_cap_count", "max_loc"}` — four fields specified
AC1 says 'exact-value asserts on all three' covering only count, max_loc, over_cap_count — near_cap_count is absent
findings.finding: `near_cap_count` is a named field in the `module_size_distribution` return dict ("within 10% of the cap") but no AC asserts its value, so a bug in that field's computation passes all acceptance criteria.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC1; ## The raw metric functions — `module_size_distribution`
scenarios: An executor sets `near_cap_count` to a hard-coded constant or implements the 10%-threshold incorrectly; all ACs still pass green
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'and `near_cap_count` equal to the number of fixture modules within 10% of the fixture cap (exact-value assert).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4 to also assert `.confidence == 'high'` and `.accruing_since == '2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00'` for each of the four registry entries.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'confidence="high"' and 'accruing_since="2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"' for all four MetricSpec entries
AC4 says 'checking `.id`/`.lens`/`.source`' — confidence and accruing_since are omitted from the assertion list
findings.finding: AC4 asserts `lens`, `source`, and `id` on each REGISTRY entry but does not assert `confidence="high"` or `accruing_since="2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"`, both of which are explicitly specified in the plan's Registration section — leaving them unverified.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC4; ## Registration into the c085 registry
scenarios: An executor sets `confidence="low"` or omits `accruing_since`; AC4 still passes green because neither field is asserted
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 assertion description with: 'asserted by iterating REGISTRY and checking `.id`, `.lens`, `.source`, `.confidence`, and `.accruing_since` for each of the four entries (exact-value asserts on all five fields).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an exact-value assert for `near_cap_count` in AC1: specify a fixture where at least one module falls within 10% of the cap and assert the returned `near_cap_count` equals the expected count.
evidence: Function spec: `{"count": <n .py modules>, "near_cap_count": <within 10% of the cap>, "over_cap_count": <exceeding the cap>, "max_loc": <largest module LOC>}`

AC1: 'returns `max_loc` equal to the largest fixture module's line count, `count` equal to the number of `.py` modules, and `over_cap_count` equal to the number exceeding the fixture cap (exact-value asserts on all three)' — `near_cap_count` is absent from the three asserted fields
findings.finding: `near_cap_count` is a named output field of `module_size_distribution` in the function spec but no acceptance criterion asserts its value, leaving it untested and its correctness unverifiable by the AC suite.
findings.location: AC line 1 / 'The raw metric functions' section — `module_size_distribution` return dict
scenarios: An implementer sets near_cap_count to a hardcoded 0 or skips the 10%-threshold logic entirely; all ACs still pass because near_cap_count is never checked.
A refactor accidentally changes the 10%-of-cap threshold to something else with no failing test.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the fixture tree so that one module is within 10% of the fixture cap (e.g., cap=100, module A=95 lines, module B=50 lines) and add to AC1: 'and `near_cap_count` equal to the number of fixture modules within 10% of the cap (exact-value assert)'.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all git subprocess calls in `git_metrics.py` (for `git log --numstat`, `git log` over the limit file, and any tree scan) route through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git`, consistent with every other git-calling module in the codebase.
evidence: Plan says functions use 'git log --numstat' and 'git log' with no mention of run_git
CHANGELOG.md:275: 'Consolidate the _git() subprocess wrappers into gitutil.run_git'
src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py:25: from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git
src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py:7: 'so run_git is that shape once, and each call site keeps its OWN return/error contract'
git_adapter.py docstring: 'Every git subprocess the reconciler runs flows through this module … built ON TOP of run_git — the one shared subprocess.run(["git", …]) shape for the whole codebase'
13+ import sites of run_git confirmed by grep across _store, _commands, _snapshot, llm
findings.finding: The plan proposes git subprocess calls (`git log --numstat`, `git log`, tree scanning) in `git_metrics.py` without specifying that they route through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` — the codebase's single, deliberate, explicitly-consolidated git subprocess wrapper. Every other git-calling module in the codebase imports and uses `run_git` (13+ confirmed call-sites: `llm/code_review/assemble.py`, `_store/sync.py`, `_store/push.py`, `_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py`, `_commands/fsck.py`, `_commands/fsck_recover.py`, `_commands/fsck_repair.py`, `_commands/verify_authorship.py`, `_commands/delete.py`, `_commands/compact.py`, `_commands/txn.py`, `_commands/init.py`, and via `git_adapter.py`). The CHANGELOG entry 'Consolidate the _git() subprocess wrappers into gitutil.run_git' records this consolidation as an explicit past refactor. Omitting `run_git` from the plan means the implementation would re-introduce raw `subprocess.run(["git", …])` calls — rebuilding what `gitutil` already provides.
findings.location: ## The raw metric functions / ## Files — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py`
scenarios: Implementer writes `subprocess.run(["git", "-C", repo_root, "log", "--numstat", ...])` directly, re-introducing the subprocess drift the consolidation eliminated.
Future changes to run_git's contract (timeout defaults, env handling, error shaping) don't apply to git_metrics.py, causing silent divergence.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: 'git_metrics.py imports `run_git` from `rebar._store.gitutil` and routes all git subprocess calls through it, e.g. `run_git(repo_root, "log", "--numstat", ...)`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `git_metrics.py`'s git subprocess calls route through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (or explicitly justify a deviation) rather than introducing a new hand-rolled `subprocess.run(["git", ...])` shape.
evidence: Plan: 'churn/refactor come from git log --numstat; cap changes from the limit file's history' — no mention of run_git
git_adapter.py docstring: 'built ON TOP of rebar._store.gitutil.run_git — the one shared subprocess.run(["git", …]) shape for the whole codebase'
gitutil.py docstring: 'consolidates the dozen hand-rolled _git() wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each'
ABSENCE: plan section 'Approach' and 'Files' contains no reference to run_git or gitutil
findings.finding: The plan introduces new git subprocess calls in `git_metrics.py` without acknowledging or committing to the codebase's established shared git wrapper (`rebar._store.gitutil.run_git`). Every other git-calling module (git_adapter.py, sync.py, push.py, assemble.py, reconciler modules) routes through `run_git` to enforce a consistent call shape (list-form argv, check/timeout/text/env posture). The plan risks hand-rolling a divergent `subprocess.run(["git", ...])` shape — the exact drift that `run_git` was created to prevent.
findings.location: ## Files / `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` — Approach section
scenarios: Implementer writes subprocess.run(["git", "-C", repo_root, "log", "--numstat", ...]) directly, diverging from the codebase convention and bypassing any future centralized timeout/env policy enforced in run_git.
A security review of git subprocess calls misses git_metrics.py because it isn't routed through the known seam.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Git subprocess calls use `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git(repo_root, "log", ...)` — the codebase's single shared git seam — rather than inline subprocess.run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define or reference the concrete context type/class (with `repo_root`, `since`, `until` attributes) that the compute adapters will receive, or coordinate with ticket 9a5a to establish this as a named interface before the adapters are written against it.
evidence: Plan states: 'The context is the small object the rebar metrics command (9a5a) passes to every compute, carrying repo_root, since, until'
Grep for 'MetricsContext' and 'class.*Context.*repo_root.*since.*until' across src/rebar returns no matches
Ticket 9a5a (the rebar metrics CLI command) is status 'open' and has no implementation yet; src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py does not exist
The existing evaluate() signature at registry.py:103 accepts `context: Any = None` — there is no type constraint or class definition for the context shape
findings.finding: The plan asserts a context object carrying `.repo_root`, `.since`, and `.until` attributes (e.g. `lambda ctx: churn(ctx.repo_root, ctx.since, ctx.until)`) that will be provided by the `rebar metrics` command (ticket 9a5a), but no such context class or type exists anywhere in the codebase. The plan's compute adapters are written against an attribute contract that is unverifiable — no `MetricsContext` (or equivalent) is defined in `src/rebar/metrics/` or the broader codebase.
findings.location: Registration into the c085 registry section; compute adapter examples
scenarios: An implementer writes `lambda ctx: churn(ctx.repo_root, ctx.since, ctx.until)` with no defined context class. When 9a5a is implemented, if the context object uses different attribute names (e.g. `ctx.start`/`ctx.end` or `ctx.range.since`) the adapters silently fail with AttributeError at runtime.
The AC-4 registry test iterates REGISTRY but never exercises the compute callables against a real context, so the attribute mismatch is not caught until 9a5a integration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` to the plan's **Files** section as an edit target (append four new `MetricSpec` entries to `REGISTRY`), since the plan body requires it but the Files list omits it.
evidence: Plan Files section lists only: 'src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py — new: the four functions above + their four MetricSpec registrations appended to src/rebar/metrics/registry.py's REGISTRY'

Reading src/rebar/metrics/registry.py confirms REGISTRY is defined there (line 133) and currently holds only three seed MetricSpecs (module_size_trend, oversized_module_count, commit_cadence_trend) — none of the four new git metric ids are present
registry.py is absent from the plan's Files list despite being a required edit target
findings.finding: `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` must be edited to append the four new `MetricSpec` entries to `REGISTRY`, but the plan's **Files** section omits it — only `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` (new) and `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py` (new) are listed. The plan body explicitly says 'their four MetricSpec registrations appended to src/rebar/metrics/registry.py's REGISTRY' but the Files section, which drives the implementer's edit set, invisibly drops this required edit.
findings.location: Files section; 'Registration into the c085 registry' section
scenarios: An implementer following only the Files section creates git_metrics.py with the four functions but never edits REGISTRY in registry.py, so the four metrics are never registered and the AC-4 assertion ('each of the four metric ids is present in REGISTRY') fails at test time.
findings.suggested_fix: In the **Files** section, change the git_metrics.py line to: `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py — new: the four raw functions.` and add a new line: `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py — edit: append the four new MetricSpec entries (ids: module_size_distribution, cap_change_events, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio) to REGISTRY.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` to the **Files** section as an edited file (four `MetricSpec` entries appended to `REGISTRY`).
evidence: Plan states: 'the four functions above + their four `MetricSpec` registrations appended to `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`'s `REGISTRY`'
Files section lists only two files: `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` and `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py`
`src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` exists and contains the `REGISTRY` list that must be mutated (confirmed by reading the file)
findings.finding: `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` must be edited to append four new `MetricSpec` entries to `REGISTRY`, but it is absent from the plan's **Files** section — only `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` and `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py` are listed there.
findings.location: Files section
scenarios: An implementer following the Files section exactly would not know to edit `registry.py`, leaving `REGISTRY` without the four new metric specs and causing AC4 (the REGISTRY assertion) to fail.
A code-review diff that only covers the listed files would miss the `registry.py` change.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add: `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` — edited: append four `MetricSpec` entries (ids `module_size_distribution`, `cap_change_events`, `churn`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`) to `REGISTRY`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling in `git_metrics.py` for git subprocess failures and missing/malformed cap file: either (a) each raw function catches these and returns a sentinel that the compute converts to None, or (b) confirm that `evaluate()` already wraps all computes in try/except and document that contract in this plan.
evidence: Plan describes happy-path returns (dict, list, float|None) but specifies no error handling for git subprocess failures
'A compute that finds no data in range returns None so evaluate() reports Unavailable' — only the no-data None path is described; exception paths are absent
'cap is read from .github/module-size-limit.txt' — no handling described if file is absent or unparseable
registry.py:103-111 behavior on exception is not described in this plan
findings.finding: The plan is silent on error handling for subprocess failures (git not available, not a git repo, corrupt history) and for missing or malformed `.github/module-size-limit.txt`. The compute lambdas would raise exceptions rather than returning None, and the plan does not state whether `evaluate()` wraps computes in try/except to convert exceptions to Unavailable.
findings.location: ## The raw metric functions / ## Registration into the c085 registry
scenarios: git binary not installed or not on PATH — git log subprocess raises FileNotFoundError
repo_root is not a git repository — git log exits non-zero
module-size-limit.txt is deleted or contains non-integer — ValueError/FileNotFoundError propagates through compute lambda
evaluate() does not catch exceptions → uncaught exception bubbles to the rebar metrics command
findings.suggested_fix: In `git_metrics.py`, wrap git subprocess calls in try/except (subprocess.CalledProcessError, FileNotFoundError, ValueError) and return None (or an empty sentinel) so the compute lambda propagates None to evaluate(). Similarly wrap the cap file read. Add a line to the plan confirming this behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either make the near-cap threshold (10%) a named constant sourced from config/the cap file context, or add a brief rationale for why 10% is an appropriate fixed value that is unlikely to need tuning.
evidence: 'near_cap_count: within 10% of the cap' — stated as a fixed implementation detail
No configuration parameter, no config file entry, and no derivation/measurement cited for the 10% value
Changeability sub-criterion: rules/thresholds expected to evolve should be configurable, not hardcoded
findings.finding: The `near_cap_count` threshold of 10% ('within 10% of the cap') is asserted as a fixed hardcoded value with no mechanism for configuration, and no rationale for why 10% is the right boundary is provided or derived from data.
findings.location: ## The raw metric functions — module_size_distribution
scenarios: Team decides 15% or 20% is a more useful warning zone — requires code change rather than config change
Different projects have different cap sizes where 10% may be too narrow or too wide

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an exact-value assertion for `near_cap_count` in AC1 (e.g. 'near_cap_count equals the number of fixture modules within 10% of the fixture cap'), covering at least one fixture module that falls in the near-cap band and one that does not.
evidence: AC1 states: 'over_cap_count equal to the number exceeding the fixture cap (exact-value asserts on all three)' — 'all three' refers to count, max_loc, over_cap_count
The raw-metric spec defines four fields: count, near_cap_count, over_cap_count, max_loc
near_cap_count has a distinct semantic (within 10% of cap) and a distinct implementation path — a wrong value would not be caught
findings.finding: The `near_cap_count` field is part of the specified `module_size_distribution` return dict but has no assertion in the acceptance criteria; the AC explicitly covers only three of the four fields (count, max_loc, over_cap_count), leaving `near_cap_count` untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 / 'module_size_distribution' return dict spec
scenarios: An implementation that computes near_cap_count incorrectly (e.g. off-by-one on the 10%-threshold boundary) passes all ACs; the field is silently wrong in production.
findings.suggested_fix: In the fixture tree, include one module whose LOC is ≥ 0.9× cap and < cap (near-cap), one above cap (over-cap), and several well below cap; assert all four fields: count, near_cap_count, over_cap_count, max_loc with exact values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `churn` and `refactor_to_addition_ratio` use `--no-merges` (or equivalent, e.g. `--diff-filter=...`) in the `git log --numstat` invocation, and add a test asserting that a range containing a merge commit still returns the correct numstat totals from non-merge commits only.
evidence: Plan: '`churn(repo_root, since, until) -> dict` → `{"insertions": <int>, "deletions": <int>}` summed from `git log --numstat` over the range'
Plan: 'a `git log --numstat` fixture yields the expected `churn` totals' (fixture uses two-commit linear history — no merge commits)
Codebase: src/rebar/attest/authorship.py:295 — '`--no-merges` skips merge commits' and line 322 uses `--no-merges` in git log calls for similar numstat-style work
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `git log --numstat` for `churn` and `refactor_to_addition_ratio` excludes merge commits. In a repo with merge commits in the range, git's default numstat output for merges is empty (or inconsistent across git versions), causing churn to be silently understated. The existing codebase's own git-log calls use `--no-merges` for exactly this reason. The unit-test fixture uses a simple two-commit linear history that won't expose this.
findings.location: The raw metric functions section — `churn` and `refactor_to_addition_ratio` descriptions; Tests section
scenarios: A repo using merge-based Git workflows (GitHub merge commits, GitLab merge commits) will have merge commits in the since..until range that contribute zero numstat lines, silently under-reporting churn.
Churn = 0 and ratio = None could be returned for an active period if all commits were merges — indistinguishable from a truly quiet period.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--no-merges` to the `git log --numstat` command in both `churn` and `refactor_to_addition_ratio`. In the plan's description of these functions, note: 'merge commits are excluded from the numstat scan (`--no-merges`) to avoid version-dependent and platform-dependent inconsistency in merged-diff stats'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the git mechanism used by `module_size_distribution` to enumerate and count lines at a non-HEAD ref (e.g., `git ls-tree -r <ref> -- src/rebar/` piped to blob reads via `git cat-file`), or explicitly scope the function to HEAD-only and remove or document the ref parameter as 'reserved / currently HEAD-only'.
evidence: Plan: 'Scans `src/rebar/**/*.py` at the ref' — 'at the ref' implies historical ref support
Plan: Approach section says only 'Distribution scans the tree at a ref against the single-sourced cap' — no git-object mechanism stated
The registered compute adapter passes only ctx.repo_root (not a ref), so the registered usage is always HEAD — the ref parameter is for direct calls only
ABSENCE: no mention of `git ls-tree`, `git archive`, `git show`, or `git cat-file` for non-HEAD tree scanning
findings.finding: The plan states `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")` scans `src/rebar/**/*.py` 'at the ref', but the Approach section only describes a filesystem glob ('scans the tree at a ref against the single-sourced cap'). For ref values other than HEAD, a plain filesystem glob reads the working tree (wrong). The plan does not specify the git mechanism for non-HEAD scanning (e.g., `git ls-tree -r <ref>` + `git cat-file`, or `git archive`), leaving the implementer to silently implement a filesystem-only path that works only at HEAD.
findings.location: The raw metric functions section — `module_size_distribution` description; Approach section
scenarios: An implementer reads the ref parameter and uses `Path(repo_root).glob('src/rebar/**/*.py')`, which always reads the working tree regardless of ref — no error, wrong data for non-HEAD refs.
A caller invoking `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref='main~10')` to compare historical module sizes gets current working-tree sizes instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git mechanism for recovering `old_limit`/`new_limit` in `cap_change_events` — e.g. `git log -p --format='%H' -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` (parse the diff hunk) or `git show <sha>:<path>` + `git show <sha>~1:<path>` per commit — so the return contract is implementable as described.
evidence: Plan: 'a list (possibly empty) of {"commit", "old_limit", "new_limit"} dicts from `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` in range'
`git log -- <path>` output: commit SHA, author, date, message only — no file content before/after
To get old_limit/new_limit the implementation must use `git log -p`, `git diff <sha>~1 <sha> -- <path>`, or `git show <sha>:<path>` — none of which are named in the plan
Absence: the plan never mentions `git show`, `git log -p`, `--patch`, or `git diff` in connection with cap_change_events
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` asserts it derives `old_limit` and `new_limit` 'from `git log`' over `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, but `git log -- <path>` only yields commit SHAs and metadata — not file content. Recovering the before/after integer values requires either `git log -p -- <path>` (diff parsing) or `git show <sha>:<path>` + `git show <sha>~1:<path>` per commit. The plan names neither command nor mechanism, leaving the core capability of this function unspecified and unverifiable. Evidence class: Contradicted — `git log` alone cannot produce the named output fields.
findings.location: ## The raw metric functions — `cap_change_events` spec; ## Tests — cap_change_events AC
scenarios: An implementer reading the plan writes `git log --format='%H' -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` and has commit SHAs but no mechanism to recover the limit values — the return contract cannot be met without additional unspecified commands.

The AC test for cap_change_events checks `old_limit`/`new_limit` values, but the plan gives no implementation guidance for how to obtain them, making the AC simultaneously a test of unspecified behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'from `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` in range' with: iterate commits touching the file via `git log --format='%H' <since>..<until> -- .github/module-size-limit.txt`; for each SHA recover content via `git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (new_limit) and `git show <sha>~1:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (old_limit); parse each as `int`. This is achievable with the existing `run_git` seam.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the git commands used to enumerate and read file contents at an arbitrary `ref` in `module_size_distribution` (e.g. `git ls-tree -r <ref> -- src/rebar/` + `git show <ref>:<path>` per file, or clarify that `ref` is always `HEAD` and on-disk scanning is intended).
evidence: Plan: '`module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD") -> dict` … Scans `src/rebar/**/*.py` at the ref.'
Plan: 'Everything is deterministic and read-only.'
Absence: no mention of `git ls-tree`, `git cat-file`, or `git show` for reading file contents at a non-HEAD ref
On-disk `glob('src/rebar/**/*.py')` + `open(path).read()` only reflects the working tree, not an arbitrary ref
findings.finding: `module_size_distribution` is specified as scanning `src/rebar/**/*.py` 'at the ref' in a read-only manner, but on-disk glob scanning only works at the working-tree state (HEAD). Scanning at an arbitrary ref requires `git ls-tree -r <ref> -- src/rebar/` to enumerate files, then `git show <ref>:<filepath>` (or `git cat-file blob`) per file to count lines. Neither command is named. The plan asserts ref-parameterized read-only behavior without specifying the git mechanism that enables it. Evidence class: Unverified — the capability exists in git but is not confirmed as the intended mechanism.
findings.location: ## The raw metric functions — `module_size_distribution` spec; `ref="HEAD"` parameter
scenarios: An implementer uses `pathlib.glob` on the working tree for any ref value — the `ref` parameter is silently ignored and the function always scans HEAD, making the ref parameter a no-op that can produce wrong results when called with a past ref.
A caller passes a historical ref to compare module sizes over time — the function returns current (HEAD) sizes instead, a silent correctness failure.

findings.finding: The `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` fields are named output fields of `module_size_distribution` in the spec, but AC1 only asserts `max_loc` and `count` — no AC asserts the correctness of `near_cap_count` or `over_cap_count`, leaving two of the four documented output fields unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 / Files section — module_size_distribution signature
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'a fixture tree containing one module at 760 LOC (within 10% of an 800-line cap) and one at 850 LOC (over cap) must yield `near_cap_count=1`, `over_cap_count=1`, `max_loc=850`, `count=2` — all four fields asserted by exact value.'

findings.finding: The Approach section and AC4 directly contradict each other on the return type of `cap_change_events` when a populated date range contains no cap-change commits: Approach says the function must 'never' return `[]`, but AC4 asserts it returns 'an empty list' in exactly that scenario.
findings.location: Approach section ('each git-metric compute returns None (never []/{}/ 0)…') vs. AC4 ('a range with no such commit returns an empty list (both asserted)')
findings.suggested_fix: Treat 'no cap-change commits in a populated range' as a legitimate measured result (an empty event list), not as 'no data'. Update the Approach section to carve out this case: `cap_change_events` returns `[]` when the range has commits but none touch the limit file, and `None` only when the repo/file is inaccessible or the range itself is invalid. This aligns with the analogy of churn returning `0` (not `None`) for a populated range with no net changes.

findings.finding: The Approach section and AC4 directly contradict each other on the return value of `cap_change_events` when a date range has commits but none touch `.github/module-size-limit.txt`. Approach says `compute` returns `None` when 'range yields no commits', implying `[]` only for populated-but-empty-match ranges; but AC4 says 'a range with no such commit returns an empty list' — which could be interpreted as either case. An executor cannot determine whether `None` or `[]` is correct when commits exist but none changed the cap file.
findings.location: Approach section ('compute returns None (never []/{}/ 0) when its range yields no commits'); AC4 ('a range with no such commit returns an empty list (both asserted)')
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify the Approach section: 'cap_change_events returns None only if .github/module-size-limit.txt is absent or cannot be read; it returns [] when commits exist in the range but none touched that file.'

findings.finding: The plan declares a semantically critical None/zero contract (empty range → None, real-zero churn → 0, absent cap file → None) but no AC exercises the empty-range or absent-cap-file paths for `churn`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio`, or `module_size_distribution` — a regression that returns 0 instead of None for an empty range would pass all ACs while violating the stated contract.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs) and Approach section ('each git-metric compute returns None (never []/{}/ 0) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test fixture with an empty date range (e.g. `since` and `until` equal to the same commit) and assert `churn(...) is None`; add a separate fixture repo without `.github/module-size-limit.txt` and assert `module_size_distribution(...) is None`. Reference the Approach-section contract in the AC text so the intent is self-documenting.

findings.finding: The plan asserts git-metric functions are 'registered into registry.py' under the c085 contract, but MetricSpec.compute is typed Callable[[Any], Any] and evaluate() calls it as spec.compute(context) — a single argument. The plan's functions take 2–3 positional args (repo_root, ref='HEAD' / repo_root, since, until), making them incompatible with the registry's single-context call slot without an unspecified adapter (partial, lambda, or context-dict protocol).
findings.location: Files section ('registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py') and Approach section ('Registered into the c085 registry')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Each function is registered via a lambda or partial that closes over `repo_root` and the date range at registration time, e.g. `compute=lambda ctx: module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")`; the `context` argument is unused for these git metrics.' Alternatively, define a `GitMetricContext` dataclass and have each compute callable accept and destructure it.

findings.finding: The git metric functions have multi-argument signatures (`module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref='HEAD')`, `churn(repo_root, since, until)`, `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)`, `refactor_to_addition_ratio(repo_root, since, until)`) but `MetricSpec.compute` is typed `Callable[[Any], Any]` and `evaluate()` unconditionally calls `spec.compute(context)` with exactly one positional argument. The plan does not specify how the multi-arg functions are adapted to this single-context contract.
findings.location: Files section (git_metrics.py function signatures); src/rebar/metrics/registry.py lines 61, 111
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Each `MetricSpec.compute` is a zero- or one-argument callable. Register git metrics using `functools.partial(module_size_distribution, repo_root=<configured_root>)` or accept a context dict and unpack `repo_root`, `since`, `until` from it — pick one pattern and state it explicitly.'

findings.finding: The plan never specifies the `lens` value for any of the four new MetricSpec entries, even though `lens` is a required (non-optional) field in the MetricSpec dataclass and its vocabulary (e.g. 'code_health', 'delivery') must match downstream consumers that segregate metrics by lens.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` / `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` lines 57-58
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to the Files section: 'All four new MetricSpec entries use `lens="code_health"` (they are module-size and churn metrics, not delivery or process metrics).'

findings.finding: The plan never specifies the `accruing_since` value for any of the four new MetricSpec entries, even though `accruing_since` is a required field (ISO-8601 string) in MetricSpec and is surfaced in every `Unavailable` response.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` / `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` lines 62, 89

findings.finding: The `compute` field of MetricSpec is `Callable[[Any], Any]` and `evaluate()` calls it as `spec.compute(context)` (exactly one argument), but the four new functions have multi-argument signatures (`module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref='HEAD')`, `churn(repo_root, since, until)`, etc.). The plan says they are 'registered into registry.py' but never specifies the wrapping/binding protocol (e.g. `functools.partial`, a lambda adapter, or a new context-dict unpacking convention).
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py`; Approach section — 'Registered into the c085 registry'; `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` line 111

findings.finding: `cap_change_events` specifies that `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` yields `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` tuples, but `git log` alone only surfaces commit SHAs (and metadata). Recovering `old_limit` and `new_limit` requires an additional step — reading the blob at `<sha>^:<path>` and `<sha>:<path>` via `git show` or `git cat-file` — which the plan never names. An implementer following the plan as written has no mechanism to produce the old/new values.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` description; also AC line: 'a commit changing `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is reported by `cap_change_events` with the old and new limit values'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `cap_change_events` description: 'For each commit SHA from `git log --pretty=format:"%H" -- .github/module-size-limit.txt`, read `new_limit` via `git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt` and `old_limit` via `git show <sha>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (skip or mark `old_limit=None` for the root commit where `^` does not exist).'

findings.finding: `cap_change_events` asserts that `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` in a date range yields `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` tuples, but `git log -- <path>` only surfaces commit metadata (SHA, author, message, timestamps) — it does NOT yield file content. Recovering `old_limit` and `new_limit` requires additional `git show <sha>^:<path>` (parent state) and `git show <sha>:<path>` (post-commit state) calls per commit returned. The plan's stated mechanism conflates commit discovery with content retrieval in a single `git log` invocation that cannot deliver all three fields.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` description; Approach section — 'cap changes from the limit file's history'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)' with: 'git log --format=%H -- .github/module-size-limit.txt in range to get commit SHAs; then for each SHA, git show <sha>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt (parent) and git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt (post) to recover old_limit and new_limit; returns [] when no commits in range, None when the file is absent from HEAD.'

findings.finding: `cap_change_events` specifies that `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt` yields `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` tuples, but `git log` (even with `-- <path>`) only surfaces commit SHAs, messages, and timestamps — it does NOT emit file content before and after the change. Recovering `old_limit` and `new_limit` requires either `git log -p` (patch output) with diff parsing, or per-commit `git show <sha>^:<file>` / `git show <sha>:<file>` calls. The plan's described approach implies a capability that `git log` alone does not provide.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` description; also AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, replace 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)' with: 'git log --format=%H -- .github/module-size-limit.txt to enumerate touching commits, then for each SHA recover old_limit = git show <sha>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt and new_limit = git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt; handle the first-commit case where sha^ does not exist (old_limit = None).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the mechanism for ref-aware file scanning in `module_size_distribution` (e.g. 'use `git ls-tree -r --name-only <ref>` piped through `git show <ref>:<path>` for line counts, not `pathlib.glob`') so the executor doesn't silently default to a HEAD-only scan.
evidence: 'Scans `src/rebar/**/*.py` at the ref' — mechanism unstated

Function signature `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")` implies ref-awareness but the plan's Approach section only says 'Distribution scans the tree at a ref against the single-sourced cap' without naming the git primitive
findings.finding: The plan says `module_size_distribution` scans `src/rebar/**/*.py` 'at the ref' but does not specify whether it uses git plumbing (e.g. `git ls-tree`/`git show`) or filesystem `pathlib.glob`. Using `pathlib.glob` silently gives HEAD state at any ref, defeating the `ref` parameter for historical queries.
findings.location: ## The raw metric functions — `module_size_distribution`; ## Approach
scenarios: An executor defaults to `pathlib.glob(repo_root / 'src/rebar/**/*.py')`, which always reflects the working-tree state, not the requested ref — silent wrong results for any ref != HEAD
A reviewer cannot verify correctness of the approach without knowing which primitive is used
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section: 'module_size_distribution reads file content via `git show <ref>:<path>` (or `git ls-tree -r --name-only <ref>` to enumerate paths), never via filesystem glob, so the ref parameter is honoured at historical commits.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
